author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

originalBook: In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.
title: Chapter 102

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 102.—231.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "You, therefore, who prefer rather to be washed with the most false of baptisms than to be regenerate, not only do not lay aside your sins, but also load your souls with the offenses of criminals.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For as the water of the guilty has been abandoned by the Holy Spirit, so it is clearly filled full of the offenses of the traditors.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: To any wretched man, then, who is baptized by one of this sort, we would say, If you have wished to be free from falsehood, you are really drenched with falsity.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If you desired to shut out the sins of the flesh, you will, as the conscience of the guilty comes upon you, be partakers likewise of their guilt.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If you wished to extinguish the flames of avarice, you are drenched with deceit, you are drenched with wickedness, you are drenched also with madness.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Lastly, if you believe that faith is identical in the giver and the receiver, you are drenched with the blood of a brother by him who slays a man.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And so it comes to pass that you, who had come to baptism free from sin, return from baptism guilty of the sin of murder."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: I should like to come to argument with those who shouted assent when they either heard or read those words of yours.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For such men have not ears in their hearts, but their heart in their ears.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Yet let them read again and again, and consider, and find out for themselves, not what the sound of those words is, but what they mean.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: First of all, to sift the meaning of the last clause, "So it comes to pass," you say, "that you who had come to baptism free from sin, return from baptism guilty of the sin of murder:" tell me, to begin with, who there is that comes to baptism free from sin, with the single exception of Him who came to be baptized, not that His iniquity should be purged away, but that an example of humility might be given us?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For what shall be forgiven to one free from sin?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or are you indeed endowed with such an eloquence, that you can show to us some innocence which yet committeth sin?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Do you not hear the words of Scripture saying, "No one is clean from sin in Thy sight, not even the infant whose life is but of a single day upon the earth?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For whence else is it that one hastens even with infants to seek remission of their sins?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Do you not hear the words of another Scripture, "In sin did my mother conceive me?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: In the next place, if a man returns a murderer, who had come without the guilt of murder, merely because he receives baptism at a murderer’s hands, then all they who returned from receiving baptism at the hands of Optatus were made partakers with Optatus.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Go now, and see with what face you cast in our teeth that we excite the wrath of kings against you.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Are you not afraid that as many satellites of Gildo will be sought for among you, as there are men who may have been baptized by Optatus?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Do you see at length how that sentence of yours, like an empty bladder, has rattled not only with a meaningless sound, but on your own head?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: To go on to the other earlier arguments which you have set before us to be refuted, they are of such a nature that we must needs allow that every one returns from baptism endued with the character of him by whom he is baptized; but God forbid that those whom you baptize should return from you infected with the same madness as possesses you when you make such a statement!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And what a dainty sound there was in your words, "You are drenched with deceit, you are drenched with wickedness, you are drenched also with madness!"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Surely you would never pour forth words like this unless you were, not drenched, but filled even to repletion with madness.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is it then true, to say nothing of the rest, that all who come untainted with covetousness to receive baptism at the hands of your covetous colleagues, or the priests of your party, return guilty of covetousness, and that those who run in soberness to the whirlpool of intoxication to be baptized return in drunkenness?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If you entertain and teach such views as this, you will have the effrontery even to quote, as making against us, the passage which you advanced some little time ago: "It is better to trust in the Lord than to put confidence in man.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It is better to trust in the Lord than to put confidence in princes."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What is the meaning of your teaching, I would ask, save only this, that we should put our confidence not in the Lord, but in man, when you say that the baptized person is made to resemble him who has baptized him?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And since you assume this as the fundamental principle of your baptism, are men to place their trust in you? and are those to place their trust in princes who were disposed to place it in the Lord?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Truly I would bid them hearken not to you, but rather to those proofs which you have urged against ourselves, ay, and to words more awful yet; for not only is it written, "It is better to trust in the Lord than to put confidence in man," but also, "Cursed be the man that trusteth in man."
